shortTitle: Pov: You're The Worst Hitman Ever

content.text: It starts in a filthy motel bathroom. Blood on your knuckles. Stale adrenaline in your breath. You’re staring at yourself in a cracked mirror, shirt torn, a tooth missing, forty bucks and a burner phone in your pocket. That’s all you’ve got to your name.
content.imageDescription: A grimy motel bathroom lit by a flickering bulb. The protagonist’s bruised, bloodied knuckles grip the sink as he gazes at his reflection in a cracked mirror. He wears a torn shirt and his mouth shows the gap of a missing tooth. A crumpled forty-dollar bill and a cheap burner phone rest on the grimy counter.

content.text: The sink is leaking. The city beyond the window never really sleeps, but right now, it’s holding its breath—waiting for you to make your next mistake. That’s how it goes. One mistake, then another. And you? You seem to be the king of mistakes.
content.imageDescription: A leaky sink drips steadily, water pooling below. Through the dirty, rain-streaked window, an endless city of neon lights glows. The air feels tense, as if the city itself is watching the protagonist, poised for him to make another blunder.

content.text: You tell yourself it’ll be different this time. You told yourself that last time too. But here you are, nursing a busted lip, flicking through unread messages from a faceless contractor—someone who trusted you to finish a job that veered from easy money to total disaster in under ten minutes.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist, in the rundown bathroom, lightly touches his swollen, split lip and grimaces. He scrolls anxiously through ominous, unread text messages on his burner phone, each notification looming with threat and disappointment.

content.text: The first time you ever pulled the trigger for a paycheck, you dropped the gun. Literally. Slipped from your sweaty palm in the stairwell, clattering so loud you froze. The mark got away. Your employer called you a liability, but didn’t kill you—too lazy, or maybe they liked seeing you squirm. That was the beginning. You bled for your first paycheck, and it bounced.
content.imageDescription: Flashback: a dimly lit stairwell. The protagonist’s sweaty hands lose their grip on a pistol, which crashes loudly down the stairs. A shadowy figure bolts away in alarm. Somewhere above, a menacing figure glares with disdain as the protagonist stands paralyzed in shame.

content.text: It’s supposed to be simple: you get a name, a location, a time. You do the work. You get paid. But nothing about your work is ever simple. Every job, something slips out of your control. The elevator jams. An alarm goes off. You get locked out of the safehouse because you lost the key. When they talk about "professionalism," they mean someone else. Not you.
content.imageDescription: A montage of jobs gone awry: an elevator panel flashing 'out of service' while the protagonist pounds it in frustration; a blaring alarm in the background; the protagonist patting empty pockets for a lost key outside a secured, ominous door. Each scene conveys mounting frustration and failure.

content.text: But you keep trying, because what else is there? You spent years with nothing—sleeping rough, learning from drunks and liars how to lie better, fight unfair, disappear into the cracks of the city. This was never the life you wanted. You just never figured out how to want anything else.
content.imageDescription: A younger version of the protagonist sleeps on a bundle of newspapers under a bridge, blending with the shadows. He's receiving advice from rough-looking drunks and streetwise figures, learning to pickpocket, throw a punch, and vanish into the urban labyrinth. His face carries longing and resignation.

content.text: Now it’s 2:12 AM. The phone vibrates with a new job. "One last favor." You stare at the words for a long time. You hope for an out—an alternate ending waiting for you if you just ignore the screen. But you know he won’t let this go.
content.imageDescription: The digital clock on the nightstand reads 2:12 AM as the burner phone buzzes brightly. The protagonist, hunched in darkness, stares long and hard at the new message: 'One last favor.' He looks both hopeful for escape and certain of being trapped again.

content.text: You call back. He says it straight—there’s a diplomat in a fancy hotel, needs to stop breathing before sunrise. Secondary objective: don’t embarrass him. Don’t let it get messy. You laugh—can’t help it. Messy is all you know. But you say yes anyway, because hope is a beast that never gets tired of biting you in the gut.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist makes a tense call from the dingy motel, his face lit by the phone screen. In an imagined overlay, a posh hotel looms, and the shadowy figure on the other line casually instructs the murder. The protagonist’s bitter, ironic smile underscores his acceptance of chaos.

content.text: You walk out into the hallway, avoiding the gaze of a housekeeping cart piled high with other people’s secrets. The city hums outside. Every step to the hotel feels like a test.
content.imageDescription: Leaving his motel room, the protagonist slips nervously past a loaded housekeeping cart stacked with towels hiding unseen items, hinting at other stories. He steps into a neon-lit hallway, the city’s vibrant rhythm pulsing beyond, every stride heavy with apprehension.

content.text: You hurry past a group of night owls in the lobby, eyes darting. Security guard at the elevator smiles too wide. You hit the 14th floor. When you reach the room, you realize you forgot your lock picks—left them on the rim of last night’s coffee cup, back in the motel. Panic spikes.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist moves rapidly past a chattering group in a ritzy hotel lobby, head low, hand clutching his pocket. The security guard at the elevator beams an exaggeratedly friendly yet suspicious grin. In front of a hotel room door on the 14th floor, a look of panic dawns as the protagonist realizes his lock picks are missing.

content.text: You check the handle. Unlocked. Luck? Or a trap? No time to wonder. You slip inside to find the mark snoring, TV flickering blue shadows across his face.
content.imageDescription: He quietly tests the doorknob—it turns. In the luxury suite, moonlight and the glow of a flickering TV bathe the sleeping diplomat sprawled on the bed, soft snores rising. The protagonist slips inside, alert yet uncertain.

content.text: You pull the silencer from your jacket, still greasy from your last failed attempt. Heart pounding. Thoughts racing. All the voices in your head—run, stay, be someone else—merge into a single buzz. You’ve done this before, but every time, your hands shake a little more.
content.imageDescription: Sweating, the protagonist screws a greasy silencer onto his sidearm, the tool trembling in his grip. A close-up of his anxious face shows doubt and fatigue. Ghostly reflections in the window suggest voices of fear and self-doubt swirling in his mind.

content.text: You line up the shot. But your foot catches on a champagne bottle and the world erupts. The mark bolts upright, sees you, and throws the bottle. It shatters against your cheek. Blood stings your eye. You hesitate—split second too long. He lunges.
content.imageDescription: As the protagonist silently takes aim, his foot bumps an empty champagne bottle that rolls and clinks. The mark is suddenly awake, eyes wide and wild; he hurls the bottle, which explodes on the protagonist’s face. Blood runs from a fresh cut as the mark leaps toward him.

content.text: It turns ugly. Furniture goes flying. You’re pinned to the floor, gasping. It takes all your strength to scramble free, bash him with the lamp, and stumble into the hallway. You leave him alive, gasping for breath—unfinished business written all over his face.
content.imageDescription: A violent struggle: overturned chairs, lamp crashing down; the protagonist lies pinned, fighting for air. In desperation, he grabs and swings a heavy lamp at the mark, scrambling to his feet and tumbling out to the hallway while the mark coughs and glares, vengeance burning in his eyes.

content.text: You left evidence everywhere. Prints, blood, a boot—your boot—lost God knows where in the room. You don’t look back. Don’t wait for the sirens. You limp down the fire stairs, two at a time, shoes scraping tile, lungs burning. At the alley, your getaway driver is gone. You never paid her. Of course you didn’t. You left your wallet in the elevator.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist flees the hotel room: a trail of bloody fingerprints on door handles, a single boot abandoned on the floor. Down a dim stairwell, he takes the steps two at a time, panting in agony. He bursts into a rain-soaked alley—no getaway car, just emptiness and mounting dread. He slaps his pockets, realizing his wallet was left behind.

content.text: Now you’re running through wet streets, one sock on, one shoe off, heart pounding like a drum in a funeral march. You duck into an all-night laundromat. Hide in the light of humming fluorescents, among basket people and forgotten children.
content.imageDescription: In a torrential downpour, the protagonist limps barefoot down the slick street, one sock soaked, the other foot bare. He slips into a shabby, neon-lit laundromat filled with weary, silent figures and children napping atop laundry baskets. The harsh fluorescent lights deepen the sense of isolation and exhaustion.

content.text: You look at your reflection in the dryer window, blurred and monstrous. You don’t look like a hitman. You look like a ghost trying to remember who he used to be. And you wonder, just for a second, what it felt like to not be afraid of your own shadow.
content.imageDescription: He stares at his warped reflection in the dryer’s curved glass: bloodied, bruised, hollow-eyed. His reflection seems more ghost than man—a haunting image of loss and fading identity, suggesting a longing for a forgotten, braver self.

content.text: When morning comes, you hear their footsteps before you see them. Two goons from the job—muscle, arms wider than your chest. Their smiles are empty. They want to know what happened to the mark. You can’t give them a straight answer, so you stall, bluff, promise to finish it tonight. You watch one of them crush a soda can with his bare hand like it’s a bug.
content.imageDescription: Morning light washes over the laundromat. Two hulking men approach, cutting intimidating figures as they step heavily across the tile. Empty, shark-like grins stretch across their faces. The protagonist fumbles for words, while one of them slowly, menacingly, crushes a soda can in his fist.

content.text: They give you twelve hours. If the job’s not done by then, you’ll be the new job. For a minute you consider walking away. Changing your name. Moving three states north to become someone nobody ever finds. You almost do it. But you don’t.
content.imageDescription: The two goons lean in, setting a metaphorical ticking clock. The protagonist stares into the distance, a fleeting daydream superimposed of him hitchhiking on a country road in disguise, contemplating escape—but ultimately, he remains rooted in place, a look of resignation settling in.

content.text: You call your only friend—the guy who owes you one free favor. He answers, half asleep, voice dripping with regret for ever knowing you. You beg for backup.
content.imageDescription: In a payphone booth near the laundromat, the protagonist dials a tattered piece of paper’s number. On the other end, his friend, groggy and annoyed, holds the phone to a tousled head, regret and concern etched on his face as he listens to the desperate plea.

content.text: The plan you improvise makes no sense, but neither does the hole you’ve dug. You meet your friend behind a greasy diner, a rain-soaked map of the city between you. You talk in fragments. Safe routes, weapons caches, exit plans. He looks scared. You are too.
content.imageDescription: Behind a battered, neon-lit diner, the protagonist and his friend huddle under a leaking awning over a map streaked with rain. Animated, fraught discussion maps their nervous energy as they plot safehouses, escape routes, and weapons stashes. Both show fear and exhaustion with furtive glances.

content.text: "Why the hell are we doing this?" he asks. You don’t have an answer. You just know that finishing the job means you get to live another day. Messing up means game over. Life, for you, is a series of extensions—never a guarantee.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist’s friend looks him in the eye, asking the hard question. The protagonist hangs his head, unable to answer. In a silent moment, the rain hammers down and the weight of their plight presses down on both.

content.text: You head back to the hotel. It’s locked down now, police tape everywhere. You slide through kitchens and service halls, stinking of old grease and someone else’s fried dreams. Your friend scans the cameras, hands shaking. You sneak into a linen closet.
content.imageDescription: The hotel glows with emergency lights; bold police tape blocks the main entrance. The duo slips in through a side door, moving through greasy, cluttered kitchens and steamy back hallways. In a cramped, musty linen closet, they crouch over a phone tapping into the security feed.

content.text: The mark is leaving—security all around him now. He looks straight at your hiding spot and mouths something: coward. You feel it deep, sharp as any bullet. Every failure, every fumble, every second thought—it’s all dragged you here.
content.imageDescription: Through a crack in the door, the protagonist sees the mark surrounded by vigilant security. The mark catches the protagonist's hidden gaze, locking eyes, and mouths 'coward.' The protagonist recoils, stung, as memories of past failures flash across his face.

content.text: You want to end this. To just get it right, for once. Crawl out of the pit you keep digging. So you follow him, two floors down. You stumble through a side door—slamming it by accident. The guards hear. You have to improvise, throwing cleaning bottles, tumbling through panic. You finally get a shot—rushed, wild. The bullet misses, ricochets. The mark screams. You’re tackled. Dragged through debris and memories. The police arrive to chaos, confusion, you gasping on the floor. Your friend runs. He’s gone. You never see him again.
content.imageDescription: A chaotic sequence: the protagonist chases after the mark, accidentally slamming a door, drawing the attention of guards. In a panic, he throws bottles to cause distraction and takes a desperate, ricocheting shot. The mark screams as security tackles the protagonist, pinning him amid spilled laundry and chaos; his friend is seen sprinting away into the confusion.

content.text: Jail smells like bleach and old sweat. You spend a night answering questions in a tiny room with flickering lights. You lie poorly. They don’t believe you, but they’ve got bigger fish. They let you go with a warning: next time, you vanish. Is that what you want? To disappear?
content.imageDescription: A stark police interrogation room: the protagonist sits slumped across a rusty metal table under harsh, flickering fluorescent lights. A detective glowers, skeptical, as the protagonist fumbles with stories and alibis. The air is thick with bleach and anxiety.

content.text: That night you sleep under a bridge, rain drumming your back into misery. Every mistake follows you, an invisible tail stitched with regret.
content.imageDescription: Homeless under a graffiti-covered bridge, the protagonist curls up miserably as rain falls heavily, soaking his makeshift bedding. The dark, wet city looms, haunting shadows suggesting the persistent presence of his regrets.

content.text: Job after job, it’s the same show. You try to get sharper, but second-guesses get in your way. You rethink every choice. Plant the bug in the wrong car. Follow the wrong man up six flights and end up face-to-face with a city councilman and his mistress—not your target, but a dead end loaded with secrets.
content.imageDescription: A comedic montage: the protagonist stares at a car with a bug hidden under the wrong fender, then chases a man up six flights only to burst into a hotel room occupied by a shocked city councilman and his scandalized mistress. Embarrassment overrides the tension.

content.text: You almost get killed for listening at the wrong door. Every new contractor gives you another chance—nobody seems to know how bad you are. Or maybe they’re all desperate for someone who’ll say yes to jobs nobody else takes.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist, ear pressed to the wrong door, recoils in panic as a shotgun blast blows splinters inches from his face. Elsewhere, shady figures hand him envelopes under flickering streetlights—everyone eager to pass him their messes.

content.text: It wears you down. On a stakeout that never ends, you start questioning everything—what brought you to this, who you were before you became a ghost living on bad luck and half-finished deeds.
content.imageDescription: In a battered old sedan parked curbside at midnight, the protagonist sits slumped over the steering wheel for hours, the city slowly passing him by. His expression is one of exhaustion and introspection, haunted by his past choices.

content.text: You remember your mother’s voice—soft, forgiving, a story read at bedtime. You remember your father’s hands—always busy, always bruised, building a life you couldn’t keep. You weren’t supposed to be the villain in your own story. Somewhere along the line, you forgot the plot.
content.imageDescription: Fade to a dimly lit childhood bedroom: the protagonist as a young child, nestled under blankets listening to his mother softly read him a bedtime story. Another image: his father's strong, bruised hands carefully repairing a broken toy. The adult protagonist sits alone, realization and loss written across his face.

content.text: Teamwork, you figure, might save you. Maybe someone else will be better at the parts you always screw up. So you try to buddy up with a pro—a woman with nerves of steel and no patience for error. The gig: sabotage a mob boss’s car before sunrise.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist stands nervously next to a tough, unflappable female partner in a dim parking lot, both whispering by the shadow of a shiny luxury car. She radiates icy competence, glaring at his every move as he fumbles with tools.

content.text: You fumble the keys, drop the bomb, almost blow your own hand off. She curses so loud half the street wakes up. Somehow you get away, but not before the boss is tipped off. Your faces end up on every back alley wanted poster. You change haircuts, grow a beard, stop sleeping.
content.imageDescription: In a frantic scene under a streetlight, the protagonist drops car keys and nearly detonates the bomb in his hand. His partner yells at him as nearby windows light up. Later, in a montage, the two see their faces pasted on wanted posters, then the protagonist changes his appearance—haircut, new beard—in a cracked mirror.

content.text: You try again with others. The hacker who never looks you in the eye. The driver with a taste for prescription pills and crashing through barricades. Every misstep is multiplied. You get shot at in a parking garage, bit by a security dog, paintballed by a twelve-year-old in a back stairway—it’s a joke, but the punchline is always on you.
content.imageDescription: Montage: working with a shy hacker glued to a laptop, then a wild-eyed driver popping pills behind the wheel as cop cars give chase. Chaos: bullets ricochet in a parking garage, a security dog bites his pant leg, and finally, a kid gleefully shoots him with a paintball gun as he races down a stairwell.

content.text: Every job gone sideways puts another hunter on your trail. Debt collectors. Betrayed clients. Once, a priest with a sawed-off and a personal vendetta. You get clever, but the city’s not that big. The walls close in. You leave messages for yourself in motels, reminders scrawled in soap: Trust no one. Don’t get too close. Don’t hope.
content.imageDescription: A shadowy parade: angry debt collectors pounding on doors, a furious client glaring from a limousine, and a wild-eyed priest wielding a sawed-off shotgun. The protagonist moves between dingy motel rooms, scrawling cryptic warnings on mirrors with a bar of soap.

content.text: Days turn into weeks. You worry that you’re losing your edge, but the truth is, you never had it. It’s not just about missing the shot. It’s about missing who you used to be: the person with dreams that didn’t end in fleeing a murder scene or patching up knife wounds with duct tape. When did you become someone who can’t walk away?
content.imageDescription: Calendar pages flip with dizzying speed. The protagonist, face drawn and weary, sits on a mattress bandaging a knife wound with duct tape, eyes hollow. In another moment, he gazes mournfully at a forgotten notebook filled with lists and dreams from a different life.

content.text: One night, everything comes to a head. Your latest job isn’t a person—it’s a message. They want you to leave a warning, burnt and loud, that can’t be traced but can’t be ignored. You can barely hold your hands steady pouring the gasoline. You light the match. In the flare, you see your reflection burning: scared, tired, alive for no good reason. The fire spreads too fast. Sirens again. You blend into the crowd, running on instinct alone, lungs full of smoke and guilt.
content.imageDescription: A deserted warehouse glows orange as the protagonist nervously pours gasoline, hands shaking. He flicks a match, flames burst up—his own haunted reflection flickers in the burning window. The fire roars out of control, sirens wail, and the protagonist melts into a fleeing crowd, coughing and frantic.

content.text: In the aftermath, you wake up coughing in a basement that smells like regret and cheap bourbon. There are photos pinned to the wall—every target you’ve missed, every time you left a mess someone else had to clean up. They want you dead now. All of them. The contractors, the marks you spared, the endless parade of middlemen who put food and fear into your hands. You’re out of chances, out of friends. And the worst part? You’re starting to think that’s how it ends for people like you.
content.imageDescription: A dim, cluttered basement. The protagonist coughs into a grimy blanket, surrounded by shadows and empty bourbon bottles. Ugly photographs and scrawled notes paper the walls—each a reminder of a bungled hit or an unfinished job. A creeping sensation of doom fills the air.

content.text: But then—one last job. A contractor you’ve never met. He offers you a way out: enough money to disappear for good. The mark? The man you used to be. Not literally. Symbolically. He wants you to kill your past, walk away from the myth of a killer you’ve never really become. You’re supposed to end it, finally. Burn the clothes, destroy the phones, take a bus to anywhere but here.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist receives a cryptic message on a new, untraceable phone—an anonymous contractor silhouetted on the screen with an offer of freedom. A symbolic montage: burning bloodstained clothes, smashing burner phones, and waiting at a bus stop while dawn breaks.

content.text: You stand in the rain outside the warehouse, clutching the last gun you’ll ever own. Inside, men wait for the real pro—the one they expect you to be. The job goes wrong immediately. You’re spotted. Bullets rain, glass shatters, you dive for cover. But this time, you don’t run.
content.imageDescription: Pounding rain blurs the figure of the protagonist, tense and determined, outside a dark warehouse. Inside: armed men lie in wait. Suddenly, gunfire erupts—windows shatter, bullets tear through crates as the protagonist throws himself behind cover but stays put, steeled for what comes next.

content.text: Something shifts inside. Call it courage, call it failure’s last gasp. You use your mistakes—throwing a busted phone as a distraction, setting off a car alarm, running zigzags through the crates. You improvise, as always. For once, it works.
content.imageDescription: Amid chaos, the protagonist hurls a broken phone to distract his pursuers. He slaps a nearby car’s hood, its alarm blaring and causing confusion. He dashes erratically between rows of crates, turning every blunder into an advantage. His expression flickers with new resolve.

content.text: You come face to face with your own reflection in a broken window, and you see it: the scared kid you buried under botched hits and second chances. You realize that maybe surviving isn’t about being good at this. Maybe it’s about finally saying no to the job. Maybe it’s about forgiving yourself for every lousy shot, every lost friend, every time you chose chaos over calm.
content.imageDescription: A cracked, rain-splashed warehouse window reveals his own reflection layered with the image of his younger, more innocent self. The protagonist stares, struggling, then softening, a tear mingling with the rain as he reaches toward self-compassion.

content.text: You don’t finish the hit. You drop the gun. Walk away, into the sunrise. No applause, no cash, no promise of safety. But the city’s awake now, and so are you. One step at a time. No more jobs. No more mistakes.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist lets the gun fall with a heavy clatter and turns his back on the carnage inside. He steps out into the dawn—sunlight washing the city in gold as he walks, alone, into a world newly awake. There is no fanfare, only aching hope.

content.text: Except maybe, just maybe, that’s the plan.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist, silhouetted against the rising sun, pauses atop a hill overlooking the city. A small, ironic smile flickers as he moves forward, determined and at peace, leaving his uncertain future open but claiming his agency at last.

victim.description: Flashback: a dimly lit stairwell. The protagonist’s sweaty hands lose their grip on a pistol, which crashes loudly down the stairs. A shadowy figure bolts away in alarm. Somewhere above, a menacing figure glares with disdain as the protagonist stands paralyzed in shame.

hitman.description: You don’t look like a hitman.

The elevator jams.
